category_title: Preschool girls performing 'sassy girl' personas through relational and physical aggression
minimal_context: Kiara is engaging in physical play with a large plastic bat on the playground with her peers.
dialogue:
TUTOR: Kiara, I see you chasing the boys with that large plastic bat. I hope you're not actually hitting people.

STUDENT: (Walks with her head down toward the teacher)

TUTOR: You're not actually hitting people with the bat, are you? You need to put it away.

STUDENT: (Walks over to the toy bin with the bat over her head and throws the bat into the bin) I don’t want to go in our castle. Come on Kim Bear ... let’s go.

TUTOR: Okay, just make sure you are playing nicely with the others.

STUDENT: (Walks quickly back, swinging hands aggressively) You can’t get it. (Walks over to the edge of the sidewalk, holding hands up on either side of face, wiggling them in a taunting manner) AHHHH HAAAA. (Walks up to Mary and pulls her arm down forcefully)

options.A: Make regulative practices explicit so the student knows exactly what to do to demonstrate competency and compliance.
options.B: Empower peers to exclude the aggressive student from play if she is being too mean and violent.
options.C: Record the student's behavioral data in scatter plots and ABC charts when the playground session finishes.
options.D: Adopt a 'leaky' and 'viscous' orientation to control, rather than instituting a much more inflexible disciplinary regime.
